title: Tool Calling is REVOLUTIONIZING AI Agents - Here's Why
sections: ScriptSection(section_id='intro', section_type='intro', title='Why Tool Calling Matters Right Now', narration_text="AI agents are hitting a wall. They're getting smarter, but they're also getting slower and more expensive to run. Here's the brutal truth: tool definitions can consume over 50,000 tokens before an agent even reads your actual request. . That's like reading an entire novel just to figure out which tool to use. . But what if AI could intelligently decide when to use tools, how to filter data, and which tools to load on-demand? That's exactly what Claude's tool calling ecosystem is solving. And this isn't some distant future feature - it's available right now through the API and Microsoft Foundry.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://claude.com/blog/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills', description=None, section_id='intro', focus_text='50,000+ tokens'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Split screen showing an overwhelmed AI processing massive tool lists', section_id='intro', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=40.800000000000004, start_time=0.0)

ScriptSection(section_id='main_programmatic', section_type='main', title='Programmatic Tool Calling: The Latency Killer', narration_text="Let me introduce you to programmatic tool calling - this is arguably the biggest shift in how AI agents work with tools. . Instead of the old approach where Claude would ask to use a tool, wait for your approval, execute it, then ask for another tool - it can now write code that calls tools directly within a code execution container. Think about what this means: you're eliminating repeated model round trips. . For multi-tool workflows, this is massive. We're talking about reducing latency by orders of magnitude because the model isn't constantly pausing to ask permission. It writes the orchestration logic - loops, conditionals, data transformations - all in one go. . The code is a natural fit for this orchestration work, and honestly, it's how it should have been from the start.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/programmatic-tool-calling', description=None, section_id='main_programmatic', focus_text='Programmatic tool calling'), VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/advanced-tool-use', description=None, section_id='main_programmatic', focus_text='code execution'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Animation showing old method vs new method - old: multiple back-and-forth exchanges, new: single efficient code block', section_id='main_programmatic', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=54.0, start_time=40.800000000000004)

ScriptSection(section_id='deep_dive_filtering', section_type='deep_dive', title='Dynamic Filtering: Context Efficiency on Steroids', narration_text="Now here's where things get really interesting. Dynamic filtering lets Claude write and execute code during web searches to filter results BEFORE they hit the context window. . This is huge for token efficiency. Instead of loading 50 search results and hoping the model figures out which ones matter, Claude can filter down to just the relevant information upfront. . The result? Higher accuracy because the model isn't drowning in noise, and lower token consumption because you're only paying for what matters. . This is the difference between an agent that feels smart and one that actually is smart. And the best part - this filtering happens automatically during the search process, so users don't even need to think about it.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://claude.com/blog/improved-web-search-with-dynamic-filtering', description=None, section_id='deep_dive_filtering', focus_text='Dynamic filtering'), VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents', description=None, section_id='deep_dive_filtering', focus_text='context efficiency'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Showing raw search results being filtered down to key data points', section_id='deep_dive_filtering', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=48.4, start_time=94.80000000000001)

ScriptSection(section_id='mcp_standards', section_type='deep_dive', title='Model Context Protocol: One Protocol to Rule Them All', narration_text="Let's talk about the Model Context Protocol - MCP. This is Anthropic's attempt to solve a massive problem in AI: fragmented integrations. . Right now, every AI tool connects to data sources differently. It's a mess. MCP provides a universal open standard for connecting AI assistants to content repositories, business tools, and development environments. . Instead of building custom integrations for every single tool, developers can now use one protocol. That's the power of standards. And this isn't just about convenience - it's about scale. The future of AI agents involves working seamlessly across hundreds or thousands of tools. You can't do that with bespoke integrations. You need a protocol. .", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol', description=None, section_id='mcp_standards', focus_text='Model Context Protocol'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Diagram showing MCP connecting AI to multiple data sources - databases, file systems, APIs', section_id='mcp_standards', focus_text=None), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Animation of an agent accessing dozens of tools through MCP', section_id='mcp_standards', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=44.4, start_time=143.20000000000002)

ScriptSection(section_id='agent_skills', section_type='deep_dive', title='Agent Skills: On-Demand Expertise', narration_text="Finally, let's discuss Agent Skills - these are reusable filesystem-based resources that provide domain-specific expertise and load on-demand. . Remember that 50,000 token problem I mentioned earlier? This is how you solve it. Instead of loading every possible tool definition upfront, Agent Skills let you define reusable expertise that loads only when needed. . This is critical for scaling. When you have thousands of tools, you can't load them all at once - you'd blow through your context window before the agent even sees the user's request. Agent Skills solve this by providing on-demand tool discovery. . The agent gets exactly the expertise it needs, exactly when it needs it. This is the future of scalable agent architectures.", visual_markers=[VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/overview', description=None, section_id='agent_skills', focus_text='Agent Skills'), VisualMarker(marker_type='screenshot', url='https://claude.com/blog/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills', description=None, section_id='agent_skills', focus_text='on-demand'), VisualMarker(marker_type='visual', url=None, description='Showing tool definitions being dynamically loaded as needed', section_id='agent_skills', focus_text=None)], estimated_duration_seconds=47.199999999999996, start_time=187.60000000000002)

ScriptSection(section_id='conclusion', section_type='conclusion', title='The Big Picture', narration_text="So what's the takeaway here? Tool calling in Claude's ecosystem isn't just about making AI slightly better - it's about fundamentally rethinking how AI agents work with tools. . Programmatic tool calling eliminates latency through direct code execution. Dynamic filtering keeps your context lean and accurate. MCP creates a universal standard for connections. And Agent Skills enable truly scalable architectures. . The key insight is this: agents need the flexibility to choose between code execution and natural language inference based on the task. Code handles orchestration beautifully. Natural language handles reasoning. The future isn't about choosing one - it's about knowing when to use each. And that's exactly what this ecosystem enables. If you're building AI agents today, these aren't nice-to-haves - they're essential. The future of AI isn't just about making models smarter. It's about making them more efficient. full_text:
AI agents are hitting a wall. They're getting smarter, but they're also getting slower and more expensive to run. Here's the brutal truth: tool definitions can consume over 50,000 tokens before an agent even reads your actual request. . That's like reading an entire novel just to figure out which tool to use. . But what if AI could intelligently decide when to use tools, how to filter data, and which tools to load on-demand? That's exactly what Claude's tool calling ecosystem is solving. And this isn't some distant future feature - it's available right now through the API and Microsoft Foundry.

Let me introduce you to programmatic tool calling - this is arguably the biggest shift in how AI agents work with tools. . Instead of the old approach where Claude would ask to use a tool, wait for your approval, execute it, then ask for another tool - it can now write code that calls tools directly within a code execution container. Think about what this means: you're eliminating repeated model round trips. . For multi-tool workflows, this is massive. We're talking about reducing latency by orders of magnitude because the model isn't constantly pausing to ask permission. It writes the orchestration logic - loops, conditionals, data transformations - all in one go. . The code is a natural fit for this orchestration work, and honestly, it's how it should have been from the start.

Now here's where things get really interesting. Dynamic filtering lets Claude write and execute code during web searches to filter results BEFORE they hit the context window. . This is huge for token efficiency. Instead of loading 50 search results and hoping the model figures out which ones matter, Claude can filter down to just the relevant information upfront. . The result? Higher accuracy because the model isn't drowning in noise, and lower token consumption because you're only paying for what matters. . This is the difference between an agent that feels smart and one that actually is smart. And the best part - this filtering happens automatically during the search process, so users don't even need to think about it.

Let's talk about the Model Context Protocol - MCP. This is Anthropic's attempt to solve a massive problem in AI: fragmented integrations. . Right now, every AI tool connects to data sources differently. It's a mess. MCP provides a universal open standard for connecting AI assistants to content repositories, business tools, and development environments. . Instead of building custom integrations for every single tool, developers can now use one protocol. That's the power of standards. And this isn't just about convenience - it's about scale. The future of AI agents involves working seamlessly across hundreds or thousands of tools. You can't do that with bespoke integrations. You need a protocol. .

Finally, let's discuss Agent Skills - these are reusable filesystem-based resources that provide domain-specific expertise and load on-demand. . Remember that 50,000 token problem I mentioned earlier? This is how you solve it. Instead of loading every possible tool definition upfront, Agent Skills let you define reusable expertise that loads only when needed. . This is critical for scaling. When you have thousands of tools, you can't load them all at once - you'd blow through your context window before the agent even sees the user's request. Agent Skills solve this by providing on-demand tool discovery. . The agent gets exactly the expertise it needs, exactly when it needs it. This is the future of scalable agent architectures.

So what's the takeaway here? Tool calling in Claude's ecosystem isn't just about making AI slightly better - it's about fundamentally rethinking how AI agents work with tools. . Programmatic tool calling eliminates latency through direct code execution. Dynamic filtering keeps your context lean and accurate. MCP creates a universal standard for connections. And Agent Skills enable truly scalable architectures. . The key insight is this: agents need the flexibility to choose between code execution and natural language inference based on the task. Code handles orchestration beautifully. Natural language handles reasoning. The future isn't about choosing one - it's about knowing when to use each. And that's exactly what this ecosystem enables. If you're building AI agents today, these aren't nice-to-haves - they're essential. The future of AI isn't just about making models smarter. It's about making them more efficient. And tool calling is how we get there. . Thanks for watching - now go build something incredible.
